stem: You are the paediatric registrar reviewing a 6-year-old non-Aboriginal boy 2 weeks after admission for Henoch-Schonlein purpura with palpable purpura on the buttocks and legs and severe abdominal pain that resolved on a short steroid course. His joint swelling and skin lesions have resolved. He is otherwise well and eating normally. His parents want to know what follow-up is needed.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 °C, HR 96, RR 20, BP 108/68 mmHg (above the 95th centile for height and age)
data_table.Examination: Fading post-inflammatory pigmentation on buttocks and lower legs, no active purpura, no joint swelling, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis blood 2+ protein 1+, urine protein:creatinine ratio 38 mg/mmol (normal under 20), serum creatinine 38 micromol/L, albumin 36 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure parents, plan routine urinalysis and blood pressure surveillance for 12 months, and refer to paediatric nephrology for ongoing assessment
options.rationale: Persistent haematuria with proteinuria above 20 mg/mmol and hypertension after HSP defines significant nephritis that requires paediatric nephrology review and 12-month surveillance per RCH guidance. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Discharge to primary care with no further follow-up given the rash has resolved and the patient is otherwise clinically well at this stage
options.rationale: Up to a third of HSP nephritis develops within 6 weeks; discharge without urinalysis surveillance misses progressive renal disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily and arrange urgent renal biopsy on the inpatient ward to confirm the diagnosis and grade severity
options.rationale: Routine prednisolone does not prevent or treat established HSP nephritis with this level of proteinuria and is not indicated without nephrotic syndrome or rapidly progressive disease. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Arrange a routine review in 6 months and rely on parental observation for blood pressure changes or new rash with no scheduled urinalysis testing in the interim
options.rationale: Parental observation cannot detect persistent microscopic haematuria or low-grade proteinuria; structured urinalysis at defined intervals is required. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Commence amlodipine 2.5 mg daily for the elevated blood pressure and review the urinalysis result in 6 weeks at the general practice clinic for further plan
options.rationale: Specific antihypertensive choice and dose in HSP nephritis should be decided by paediatric nephrology after biopsy decisions; ACE inhibitors are preferred when proteinuria is the driver. (Source: KHA-CARI Paediatric Nephrology Guidelines)

explanation.summary: HSP is a small-vessel IgA vasculitis where extra-renal features usually resolve in weeks but nephritis can develop or worsen over the following 6 months. Australian practice is structured urinalysis and blood pressure surveillance at 1, 2, 4, 6 and 12 months from diagnosis. Nephrology referral is triggered by persistent proteinuria, hypertension, or rising creatinine. Renal biopsy and immunosuppression are reserved for severe or rapidly progressive nephritis.
key_points: HSP nephritis can develop or worsen for up to 6 months after the rash resolves
Structured urinalysis and BP at 1, 2, 4, 6 and 12 months is standard surveillance
Refer to paediatric nephrology for persistent proteinuria, hypertension or raised creatinine
Routine corticosteroids do not prevent or treat established HSP nephritis
ACE inhibitors are the preferred antihypertensive when proteinuria is present, started under specialist guidance
explanation.pearls: Sustained proteinuria over 6 months or any nephrotic-range proteinuria in HSP triggers same-day nephrology referral because biopsy-proven crescentic disease has the highest long-term risk of chronic kidney disease.

subtopic_detail: Acute gastroenteritis in a toddler with mild dehydration
stem: You are the paediatric emergency resident reviewing a 14-month-old non-Aboriginal girl with 36 hours of watery non-bloody diarrhoea and three episodes of vomiting earlier in the day. She has been refusing breastfeeds but accepts sips of water. She lives at home with vaccinated siblings, attended childcare in the last week, and had her rotavirus immunisations on schedule. She is alert, plays in mother's lap and has cried tears during examination. Her parents are worried about her not eating solids.

data_table.Vital signs: T 37.6 °C, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 99% room air, capillary refill 2 seconds, weight 9.2 kg (down from 9.7 kg one week ago at the child health nurse visit)
data_table.Examination: Alert and playful, mucous membranes mildly dry, eyes not sunken, anterior fontanelle closed, abdomen soft non-tender, bowel sounds active, no rash

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer single-dose ondansetron 2 mg orally, observe for 30 minutes, then commence oral rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg per hour for the first hour
options.rationale: Ondansetron is reserved for children who cannot retain oral fluids due to vomiting; this child has not vomited in the past several hours and tolerates sips. The bolus rate suggested is also faster than guideline. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Insert a peripheral cannula and commence intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride with 5% glucose at maintenance plus deficit replacement over 24 hours
options.rationale: IV fluids are second-line after failed oral or nasogastric rehydration in mild dehydration; first-attempt cannulation is unnecessarily invasive when oral fluid is tolerated. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Commence oral rehydration solution at 50 mL/kg over 4 hours given in 5 mL aliquots every 5 minutes, continue breastfeeding, and review at 4 hours
options.rationale: Australian guidelines recommend oral rehydration as first-line for mild to moderate dehydration with 50 mL/kg over 4 hours plus maintenance, continuing breastfeeding throughout. Review at 4 hours assesses fluid intake and tolerance. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Insert a nasogastric tube and commence rehydration solution at 25 mL/kg per hour for 4 hours given the failure of breastfeeding and the recent weight loss noted
options.rationale: NG rehydration is appropriate when oral feeding fails despite encouragement; this child has not yet been trialled on oral rehydration solution in titrated aliquots. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

options.text: Prescribe loperamide 0.2 mg/kg as a single dose to reduce stool losses and discharge home with parental advice to push high-sugar fluids such as apple juice
options.rationale: Antidiarrhoeals are contraindicated in young children due to ileus and toxic megacolon risk; high-sugar fluids worsen osmotic diarrhoea. (Source: RCH CPG: Gastroenteritis)

explanation.summary: Australian gastroenteritis management starts with structured oral rehydration solution given in small frequent aliquots, with breastfeeding continued. Mild to moderate dehydration is treated with 50 mL/kg over 4 hours plus maintenance. Ondansetron and nasogastric rehydration are reserved for failure of oral therapy. Intravenous fluids are second-line for severe dehydration or failure of enteral routes. Antidiarrhoeals are not used in children.
key_points: Oral rehydration solution 50 mL/kg over 4 hours plus maintenance is first-line for mild to moderate dehydration
Continue breastfeeding throughout rehydration
Ondansetron is reserved for children unable to tolerate oral fluids due to vomiting
Nasogastric or intravenous fluids escalate when oral fails or dehydration is severe
Loperamide and other antidiarrhoeals are contraindicated in children
explanation.pearls: Bloody diarrhoea, prolonged duration over 10 days, or recent travel triggers a stool culture and PCR including shiga-toxin producing E. coli, because empirical antibiotics in E. coli O157 increase haemolytic uraemic syndrome risk.

subtopic_detail: Positive newborn screen with raised immunoreactive trypsinogen
stem: You are the paediatric registrar at the regional CF service called by a community midwife about a 12-day-old non-Aboriginal infant whose newborn screening card returned a raised immunoreactive trypsinogen with two CFTR mutations identified on the second-tier panel. The infant is asymptomatic, breastfeeding well and has regained birth weight. There is no family history of cystic fibrosis. The parents have just been told there is a problem with the screening test and are anxious.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.9 °C, HR 138, RR 44, SpO2 99% room air
data_table.Examination: Well-perfused, chest clear, abdomen soft with normal stools, no nasal polyps, no failure to thrive
data_table.Investigations: Newborn screening IRT raised, CFTR genotype Phe508del / G551D, confirmatory tests not yet performed

lead_in: What is the single best next step?

options.text: Refer to the general paediatric clinic in 6 weeks for routine review, with no specific cystic fibrosis testing planned until symptoms appear or weight gain falters in early infancy
options.rationale: A 6-week non-CF-specific review misses the early-intervention window; sweat testing within the first weeks of life is the Australian standard after a positive screen. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement)
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement

options.text: Reassure the parents that the raised immunoreactive trypsinogen is a common false positive and arrange a repeat newborn screening card in 4 weeks before further action
options.rationale: Two pathogenic CFTR mutations on the second-tier panel makes false positivity extremely unlikely; delay risks pancreatic insufficiency presenting as failure to thrive. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement)
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement

options.text: Commence pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy 2000 units lipase per 120 mL feed and arrange a chest X-ray plus high-resolution CT within the week to assess for bronchiectasis
options.rationale: Pancreatic enzymes are started after sweat test confirmation and evidence of fat malabsorption; routine chest imaging in an asymptomatic neonate is not indicated. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement)
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement

options.text: Arrange urgent referral to the tertiary paediatric cystic fibrosis clinic for sweat chloride testing and structured family counselling within 1 week
options.rationale: Two CFTR mutations on the newborn screen still require sweat chloride confirmation and multidisciplinary CF clinic linkage; early diagnosis improves nutritional and respiratory outcomes. (Source: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement)
source_refs: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement

options.text: Initiate oral azithromycin 5 mg/kg three times weekly for prophylaxis against Pseudomonas aeruginosa colonisation and order nasal swab surveillance every 6 weeks
options.rationale: Azithromycin and surveillance swabs are introduced later in CF care under specialist guidance; they are not the first step at diagnosis confirmation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Respiratory)

explanation.summary: Australian newborn screening uses raised immunoreactive trypsinogen followed by a second-tier CFTR genotype panel; two mutations strongly suggest cystic fibrosis but sweat chloride remains the confirmatory test. Early referral to a tertiary CF clinic enables sweat testing, family counselling, dietitian and physiotherapy linkage and surveillance microbiology. Pancreatic enzyme replacement and CFTR modulator decisions occur after confirmation. Time to diagnosis matters: early intervention improves growth and lung function.
key_points: IRT plus two CFTR mutations on newborn screening is highly suggestive but not diagnostic
Sweat chloride over 60 mmol/L confirms cystic fibrosis
Urgent referral to a tertiary CF clinic with multidisciplinary team is the next step
Pancreatic enzymes start after confirmation and evidence of malabsorption
CFTR modulators such as elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor are increasingly used from young ages in eligible genotypes
explanation.pearls: Sweat chloride values of 30 to 59 mmol/L are equivocal in infants under 6 months and require repeat testing and CF specialist input; values 60 mmol/L and above confirm diagnosis even with only one or no identified CFTR mutation.

sources.label: Cystic Fibrosis Australia Newborn Screening Position Statement

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 31-year-old non-Aboriginal woman in her first pregnancy at 33+4 weeks gestation in the day assessment unit with persistently elevated blood pressures detected at her community antenatal visit. She is asymptomatic with no headache, visual disturbance or epigastric pain. There is no proteinuria, normal foetal movements, and a reassuring CTG. Her booking blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 152/98 mmHg (repeated 3 times at least 4 hours apart, all readings 148/96 or higher), HR 82, RR 16, T 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height appropriate, foetal heart 142 bpm, no peripheral oedema beyond ankles, no hyperreflexia, no epigastric tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis no protein, urine protein:creatinine ratio 18 mg/mmol, FBC platelets 232 x10^9/L Hb 116 g/L, UEC creatinine 64 micromol/L, LFTs ALT 22 U/L, urate 0.30 mmol/L, CTG normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence aspirin 150 mg daily as primary prevention for pre-eclampsia, withhold antihypertensive treatment, and arrange urgent uterine artery Doppler scan within 48 hours of presentation
options.rationale: Aspirin pre-eclampsia prophylaxis is started before 16 weeks gestation; at 33 weeks it provides no benefit and does not treat established hypertension. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Admit for inpatient observation, commence intravenous hydralazine 5 mg boluses titrated to BP under 140/90 mmHg, and arrange magnesium sulfate infusion to prevent eclampsia
options.rationale: IV hydralazine and magnesium sulfate are reserved for severe hypertension at 160/110 or higher or for established pre-eclampsia with severe features; this woman has neither. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Withhold antihypertensive treatment, organise twice-weekly community midwife BP checks, and review in the antenatal clinic in 2 weeks for a treatment decision
options.rationale: Sustained BP over 140/90 in pregnancy mandates treatment to reduce the risk of progression to severe hypertension, stroke and placental abruption. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Commence oral atenolol 50 mg daily, plan repeat day assessment in 1 week, and arrange a growth scan at 36 weeks given the mild blood pressure elevation noted
options.rationale: Atenolol is associated with foetal growth restriction in pregnancy and is not recommended in Australian guidelines; labetalol, oral nifedipine or methyldopa are preferred. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Commence oral labetalol 100 mg twice daily, plan day assessment review in 48 hours, and counsel on pre-eclampsia warning symptoms and self-monitoring
options.rationale: Persistent BP over 140/90 mmHg without proteinuria after 20 weeks meets gestational hypertension criteria; SOMANZ recommends antihypertensive treatment when sustained BP exceeds 140/90, with labetalol as first-line in Australia. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Australian SOMANZ guidance defines gestational hypertension as new-onset BP 140/90 mmHg or higher after 20 weeks without proteinuria or end-organ involvement. Treatment with oral labetalol, nifedipine or methyldopa starts once BP is sustained above 140/90, aiming for 110 to 140 systolic and 80 to 85 diastolic. Severe hypertension (160/110) requires admission and parenteral therapy. Aspirin for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis is commenced before 16 weeks in at-risk pregnancies, not at presentation of established hypertension.
key_points: Gestational hypertension is sustained BP 140/90 or higher after 20 weeks without proteinuria
Treat sustained BP 140/90 or higher with labetalol, nifedipine or methyldopa
Severe hypertension 160/110 or higher requires admission and IV therapy
Atenolol is avoided due to foetal growth restriction risk
Aspirin pre-eclampsia prophylaxis is started before 16 weeks for at-risk pregnancies, not at later presentation
explanation.pearls: BP target in pregnancy under antihypertensive treatment is 110 to 140 systolic and 80 to 85 diastolic per SOMANZ; tighter targets do not improve foetal outcomes and risk placental hypoperfusion.

subtopic_detail: Antenatal corticosteroid timing in threatened preterm labour
stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 29-year-old non-Aboriginal woman at 30+2 weeks gestation in her second pregnancy who presents to birth suite with regular painful contractions every 5 minutes and intact membranes. Her first pregnancy ended at 38 weeks with normal vaginal birth. She has not previously received corticosteroids in this pregnancy. The CTG is reassuring, and there is no antepartum haemorrhage. Speculum examination shows the cervix is 2 cm long and 2 cm dilated.

data_table.Examination: Uterine activity 3 in 10 minutes lasting 45 seconds, cervix 2 cm long and 2 cm dilated, membranes intact, foetus cephalic, foetal heart 140 bpm reactive
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, MSU clear, foetal fibronectin positive, ultrasound estimated foetal weight 1450 g (45th centile), cervical length 18 mm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg now and again in 24 hours, commence intravenous magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection, and arrange transfer to a tertiary centre
options.rationale: Threatened preterm birth at 30+2 weeks warrants two doses of betamethasone 24 hours apart to mature foetal lungs, magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection before 30 weeks would be missed but is still given under 32 weeks at imminent birth, and tertiary transfer ensures neonatal capability. (Source: RANZCOG Antenatal Corticosteroids Statement)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Preterm labour and birth

options.text: Administer intramuscular betamethasone 11.4 mg now, withhold tocolysis and magnesium sulfate as she is over 30 weeks, and continue routine ward monitoring with CTG every 4 hours
options.rationale: Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection is recommended in Australia up to 32+0 weeks when birth is anticipated within 24 hours; withholding it misses the neurodevelopmental benefit. (Source: RANZCOG Antenatal Corticosteroids Statement)

options.text: Administer two doses of intravenous dexamethasone 6 mg 12 hours apart and arrange immediate caesarean section under regional anaesthesia given the unfavourable cervical findings noted
options.rationale: The Australian standard for antenatal corticosteroids is betamethasone IM in two doses 24 hours apart; dexamethasone is an alternative with a different dosing schedule and IV use is non-standard. Immediate caesarean is not indicated without a foetal or maternal compromise trigger. (Source: RANZCOG Antenatal Corticosteroids Statement)

options.text: Commence oral nifedipine 20 mg as tocolysis, withhold corticosteroids until contractions stop, and review the cervical length and corticosteroid plan in the morning
options.rationale: Corticosteroids must be given as soon as preterm birth is anticipated within 7 days; the first dose still confers benefit even if birth occurs before the second dose. (Source: RANZCOG Antenatal Corticosteroids Statement)

options.text: Withhold corticosteroids as she is over 30 weeks gestation, commence vaginal progesterone 200 mg nightly, and discharge home with planned outpatient review in 1 week
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is preventative and started earlier in pregnancy; corticosteroids are recommended up to 34+6 weeks for threatened preterm birth in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG Antenatal Corticosteroids Statement)

explanation.summary: Antenatal corticosteroids reduce neonatal respiratory distress, intraventricular haemorrhage and mortality when given before preterm birth. Australian guidance is two doses of IM betamethasone 11.4 mg, 24 hours apart, ideally completed in the 24 hours to 7 days before birth, and offered up to 34+6 weeks. Magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection is given up to 32+0 weeks when birth is anticipated within 24 hours. Tertiary in-utero transfer is preferred over ex-utero transfer for births under 32 weeks.
key_points: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, 2 doses 24 hours apart, is the Australian standard antenatal corticosteroid
Benefit extends from 24 hours to 7 days before birth
Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection is offered up to 32+0 weeks when imminent preterm birth is expected
In-utero transfer to a tertiary centre is preferred for anticipated birth under 32 weeks
Tocolysis with nifedipine can delay birth 48 hours to allow corticosteroid effect but does not improve neonatal outcomes alone
explanation.pearls: A single repeat (rescue) course of betamethasone may be considered if the initial course was over 7 days ago and birth is again imminent before 32 weeks, but more than one repeat course is associated with reduced birth weight and head circumference.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guidelines: Preterm labour and birth
